advantages: One declarative file replaces `setup.py` + `setup.cfg` + `MANIFEST.in` + per-tool config files.
Standardised by PEPs (517, 518, 621, 660) — works with every modern build backend.
Backend-agnostic `[project]` table — moving between setuptools/hatch/flit/poetry-core is mostly painless.
Centralised tool config in `[tool.*]` keeps the repo root tidy.
Enables isolated, reproducible builds via `python -m build`.
disadvantages: TOML is less expressive than Python; complex build logic still needs a backend plugin or a small `setup.py`.
Many backends, slightly different `[tool.*]` configs — mild fragmentation.
Lock files are separate; `pyproject.toml` alone doesn't give reproducible installs.
Older docs and tutorials still show `setup.py`; you have to translate when reading them.
Backend choice locks in some workflow assumptions (e.g. `poetry-core` ties you to Poetry's lock model).
